planting.note: Transplant small rhizome divisions in spring in humus-rich, moist, slightly acidic soil in full shade; it self-sows to naturalize where conditions are right.

directions: Transplant rhizome divisions into consistently moist, humus-rich, acidic soil in full shade; this delicate woodland plant with clover-like three-part leaves and white or pale pink flowers naturally carpets the floor of coniferous and mixed forests in temperate regions around the world. The leaves have a bright, tart, lemony flavor from oxalic acid and have been used as a trail snack, salad green, and flavoring for centuries — the same sour flavor as rhubarb or sorrel but milder. Consume in moderation, as large quantities of oxalic acid can interfere with calcium absorption. The plant goes dormant in summer heat; it is best suited to northern gardens and cool maritime climates.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring in partial shade; space at 12 inches for coverage within one full growing season as plants spread by both stolons and seed.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; green and gold is one of the finest native groundcovers for zone 8–9 southern gardens, producing bright yellow daisy flowers from early spring through late fall — an extraordinarily long bloom season for a shade plant. The semi-evergreen foliage forms a dense, weed-suppressing mat that spreads by stolons and self-sows without becoming invasive. It tolerates the root competition and dry summer shade under mature trees better than most groundcovers. The northern ecotype (var. virginianum) has a more upright habit, while the southern ecotype (var. australe) is more prostrate and heat tolerant — choose the southern ecotype for zone 8–9 gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in full sun and lean, well-drained soil; avoid fertilizing, which produces tall floppy growth instead of compact flowering plants.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; aromatic aster is one of the best fall-blooming native asters for difficult dry conditions, covering itself with lavender-blue flowers from September through hard frost and attracting migrating monarch butterflies and specialist aster bees. Pinch tips once in late spring to promote bushy, self-supporting growth; plants grown in rich soil or shade become floppy and bloom poorly. The leaves have a pleasant sticky, resinous aroma when brushed. Divide every 3 years in spring to keep clumps vigorous; excellent at the front of a drought-tolerant native plant garden or in a parking strip.

planting.note: Surface sow seed in spring in partial to full shade; plants establish readily and self-sow to naturalize. Not appropriate near livestock — the plant's toxins pass through cow's milk and historically caused 'milk sickness'.

directions: Surface sow seed or plant divisions in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; white snakeroot fills a critical niche as a shade-tolerant late-season bloomer, producing clouds of white fluffy flowers from August through October when pollinators most need late-season forage in shaded areas. It naturalizes freely by self-seeding under trees where few other plants bloom in fall. Note that all parts of the plant are toxic to livestock and humans — the toxin tremetol accumulates in milk of cows that graze on it, historically causing 'milk sickness' in settlers. Do not plant near pastures or where children might graze on it; it is safe as an ornamental in a garden setting.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in early spring in consistently moist soil; this plant grows best with reliable moisture and will struggle without it.

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, rich, slightly acidic soil in full sun to light shade; queen of the prairie is a magnificent native for rain gardens, pond edges, and any low area with reliable summer moisture. The enormous clouds of fluffy, peach-pink flowers on tall stems in midsummer are among the most dramatic native perennial displays available and attract native bees in large numbers. The plant spreads by rhizome to form large colonies and is not suited to small gardens or dry sites. Divide every 4–5 years in spring; each division should include a generous section of rhizome with multiple crowns. Cut back in late fall after frost.
